notes.note: A jab at Catius the Insubrian Epicurean, who coined the Latin spectra to render Epicurus's eidola, the thin films Epicurean physics held that objects continually shed to produce sense-images and the appearances of the absent or dead.
Cassius and Cicero had been bantering over philosophy; Cassius threatens to retaliate for the 'Catian spectres' by inflicting on Cicero a flood of boorish Stoic doctrine in his next letter.

notes.anchor: left the city in his general's cloak
notes.note: The paludatus, the scarlet military cloak a magistrate donned on leaving Rome to take up a provincial command; Pansa was setting out to govern Cisalpine Gaul in 45 BC.
Catius and Amafinius were the early Latin popularizers of Epicureanism; Cassius, himself an Epicurean, dismisses them as crude vulgarizers who debased the master's teaching.

notes.anchor: bought up all the goods at once
notes.note: A pun on bona ('goods'/'the good'): P. Cornelius Sulla, the speculator who had recently died, sidestepped the philosophers' quarrel over the supreme good by buying up confiscated estates wholesale at Caesar's auctions.

notes.anchor: want for a buyer at his auctions
notes.note: The sector was the bidder at auctions of confiscated property; Cassius jokes grimly that Caesar's proscription-style sales will not lack a successor to P. Sulla once Sulla's son takes up the trade.

Gnaeus Pompeius the Younger, the elder son of Pompey the Great, then leading the surviving Pompeian army in Spain; Cassius fears his victory would replace Caesar's clemency with a vindictive new master. He was beaten at Munda weeks later.
